title: Panoramic Exposures, Multiple-Film Loading, and Specimen Configuration
narrationScript: Imagine a thirty six inch vessel girth weld. Shot from outside with the double wall single view technique it would take a long string of exposures, each one set up, shot, and broken down. Now put the source in the middle of the vessel, on the axis, wrap film cassettes end to end around the whole circumference, and make one exposure. Every inch of that weld is radiographed simultaneously, single wall, with the beam striking the weld squarely all the way around. That is panoramic radiography, and when the part allows it, nothing else comes close for productivity. The geometry is the whole trick. The source must sit on the centerline, because every point on the weld then has the same source to film distance, the same beam angle, and therefore the same density on film. Let the source drift off center and the near side of the circumference runs dark while the far side runs light, and the films on one side can fail density limits while the other side fails coverage. Centering fixtures, spiders, and careful measurement are not optional refinements. They are the technique. The source itself must radiate in all directions, which a bare gamma source does naturally. For X-ray work there are panoramic tubes with rod shaped anodes that throw a continuous belt of radiation three hundred sixty degrees around the tube axis. Pipeline crawlers carry exactly this kind of equipment down the bore of a pipeline, parking at each girth weld on command and firing one panoramic shot while the film waits outside. Because one exposure covers the full circle, image quality must be proven around the full circle too, so panoramic shots carry multiple image quality indicators spaced around the circumference, commonly at least three of them set one hundred twenty degrees apart, along with location markers that tie each film back to its station on the weld. Now, multiple film loading. There is nothing that says a cassette holds only one sheet. Load two films and every exposure does double duty, and there are three classic reasons to do it. First, covering a thickness range. Put a fast film and a slow film in the same cassette and shoot a part whose sections vary widely in thickness. Behind the thick sections, where little radiation gets through, the fast film builds a readable density while the slow film stays thin. Behind the thin sections, where plenty of radiation gets through, the fast film overshoots into blackness but the slow film lands right in the readable window. One exposure, two films, the whole thickness range covered. Second, duplicate records. Two films of the same speed exposed together produce two originals, one for the customer and one for the file, with no second exposure. Third, composite viewing. Some codes allow two films exposed together to be interpreted on the viewer as a superimposed pair, their densities adding. Each film alone is lighter than a normal radiograph, but together they reach the required density, which buys exposure flexibility on difficult shots. When films are read this way, the code sets a minimum density for each individual film as well as for the pair, so neither sheet is just a foggy passenger. Finally, specimen configuration, which is the polite way of saying that parts are rarely flat plates. The shape of the specimen drives every technique decision you make. Keep the film as close to the part as the shape allows, aim the beam as squarely as you can through the section of interest, and try to keep the path length through the material constant across the area each film covers. A cylinder shot across its diameter presents a short path at the center and a long path at the edges, which is exactly why curved parts get film curved with them and why coverage per shot is limited. Abrupt thickness changes, like a boss on a plate or a stepped shaft, call for the latitude tools, higher energy, multiple film loading, or separate exposures per thickness. Complex castings may need a series of shots at different orientations, each covering the region it presents favorably, with masking or blocking material packed around thin edges to soak up the scatter that would otherwise fog the image. Plan the geometry around the part, not the other way around, and write the shot plan down before anyone pushes a source out of the camera.
imagePrompt: A radiography crew guides a crawler-mounted panoramic X-ray unit into the open end of a large steel pipeline at dusk while two technicians tape film cassettes end to end around the outside of a girth weld.

sections.heading: Panoramic Radiography: One Exposure, Full Circumference
sections.body:
Panoramic radiography puts the source **on the centerline** of a pipe or vessel, wraps film cassettes end-to-end around the **outside** of the full circumference, and exposes the entire girth weld in **one shot**. It is a single-wall, single-image technique — the beam crosses one wall, square-on, everywhere at once — so it delivers single-wall quality with unmatched productivity: one exposure replaces the three or more setups a DWSI approach would need, and on production work such as pipelines and vessel rounds that difference dominates the schedule.

The requirements are strict because the advantage is pure geometry:

- **Source centered on the axis.** Every point of the weld then sees the same source-to-film distance and beam angle, so density comes out uniform around the circle.
- **Radiation in all directions.** A bare gamma source emits spherically by nature; panoramic X-ray sets use **rod-anode tubes** that emit a continuous 360° radial belt of radiation. Pipeline **crawlers** carry such units down the bore, parking at each weld to fire one panoramic exposure.
- **Quality proven around the circle.** Because one exposure covers everything, IQIs are spaced around the circumference — commonly at least **three, 120° apart** — with location markers tying each film to its station.

sections.caption: Panoramic exposure: a centered source radiates through one wall to film wrapped around the full circumference, with IQIs spaced 120 degrees apart.

sections.heading: When Centering Fails
sections.body:
Centering is the technique. Let the source sit off-axis and the geometry unravels in two directions at once: the **near side** of the circumference, at shorter distance, runs **dark**, while the **far side** runs **light** — inverse square law acting around the circle. Films on one side fail maximum-density or fog into unreadability while the opposite side misses minimum density, and the beam no longer strikes the weld squarely, so image quality degrades unevenly as well.

Field discipline that prevents it:

- Use **centering fixtures or spiders** to hold the source or guide-tube tip on the axis; measure, don't eyeball.
- On crawler work, verify the head position against the weld (external magnetic locators or command systems) before firing.
- Read the films as a set: a smooth density gradient around the circumference is the signature of an off-center source, not of the weld.

Where the part or closure prevents true centering, the exposure stops being panoramic — coverage must then be re-planned as a series of offset shots with the coverage and IQI rules applied per shot.

sections.heading: Multiple-Film Loading
sections.body:
A cassette can carry **two (or more) films**, and loading it that way turns one exposure into several radiographs. Three classic uses:

- **Covering a thickness range.** Load a **fast** film and a **slow** film together and shoot a part with widely varying sections. Behind **thick** sections, little radiation arrives — the fast film builds readable density while the slow film stays thin. Behind **thin** sections, the fast film overshoots to black but the **slow** film lands in the readable window. One exposure covers the whole range that no single film could span.
- **Duplicate records.** Two films of the **same** speed exposed together yield two original radiographs — customer copy and file copy — with no second exposure.
- **Composite viewing.** Where the governing code permits, two films exposed together may be **interpreted superimposed**, their densities adding on the viewer. Each film alone is lighter than a single-film radiograph, but the pair meets the required density. Codes that allow this set a **minimum density for each film individually** as well as for the composite, so neither sheet rides along unreadably thin.

Keep screen arrangement in mind: with two films between lead screens, the films also screen each other, and relative speeds shift slightly — base the technique on a confirmed exposure, not on single-film chart values alone.

sections.caption: Two-film loading on a stepped part: the fast film reads the thick sections while the slow film reads the thin sections of the same exposure.
Multiple-film loading: what goes in the cassette and why.
rows: Fast film images thick sections; slow film images thin sections — one exposure covers both
Two original radiographs from one exposure (customer + file)
Densities add on the viewer; each film must also meet its own minimum density per code

sections.heading: Specimen Configuration Drives the Technique
sections.body:
Parts are rarely flat plates, and the specimen's shape is the first input to every technique decision.

- **Keep film close and beam square.** Place the film as close to the surface of interest as the shape allows and aim the central beam as nearly perpendicular to the section (and film) as practical — angled beams distort and elongate images.
- **Manage path length.** A cylinder shot across its diameter presents a short path at the center and a rapidly lengthening path toward the edges; that is why curved parts get curved film, why coverage per exposure is limited, and why diagnostic-length rules exist.
- **Abrupt thickness changes** — bosses, steps, transitions — call for the latitude tools: higher energy, multiple-film loading, or separate exposures per thickness range.
- **Complex castings and weldments** need a planned series of orientations, each presenting one region favorably, with **masking and blocking** (lead, shot bags, barite putty) packed around thin edges to absorb the scatter that would otherwise fog adjacent areas.
- **Branch, nozzle, and fillet welds** rarely allow ideal geometry; expect angled shots chosen to lay the weld out along the film with the least superimposition.

Write the shot plan — source positions, film stations, markers — before the first exposure, not after the first reshoot.

sections.title: Match the technique to the part
sections.body: Centered source + wrapped film = **panoramic**: one single-wall exposure, full circumference, IQIs spaced around the circle. Wide thickness range = **multi-film loading** or higher energy. Complex shape = planned multiple orientations with masking. The part's configuration — access, curvature, thickness profile — picks the technique; the radiographer's job is to read the part first and the exposure chart second.

figures.title: Multiple-film loading for a stepped part

checkQuestions.question: A 30 in. vessel girth weld is shot panoramic with the source centered on the axis. How many exposures does complete circumferential coverage require?
options: One
Two, 180 degrees apart
Three, 120 degrees apart
One per film cassette
checkQuestions.explanation: Panoramic geometry exposes the entire circumference simultaneously — film wraps the full weld and the centered source irradiates it all in a single exposure. That productivity is the technique's main advantage.

checkQuestions.question: Panoramic films from a pipe weld show smoothly increasing density from one side of the circumference to the other. The most likely cause is:
options: Exhausted developer
The source was off-center, so source-to-film distance varied around the circle
The film was too slow
Backscatter from the floor
checkQuestions.explanation: An off-axis source sits closer to one side of the circumference than the other; by the inverse square law the near side runs dark and the far side light, producing a smooth density gradient around the weld.

checkQuestions.question: What makes a rod-anode X-ray tube suited to panoramic work?
options: It produces a narrow, highly collimated beam
It emits a continuous 360-degree radial belt of radiation around the tube axis
It operates without electrical power
It eliminates the need for film
checkQuestions.explanation: Panoramic exposures need radiation in all radial directions at once. A rod-anode (panoramic) tube emits a 360-degree belt of X-rays, which is why pipeline crawlers carry exactly this design.

checkQuestions.question: A casting has 0.5 in. and 1.5 in. sections in the same field of view. A fast and a slow film are loaded in one cassette and exposed together. Which film images which section?
options: The slow film images the thick section; the fast film images the thin section
The fast film images the thick section; the slow film images the thin section
Both films image both sections identically
Neither film can image the thick section
checkQuestions.explanation: Behind the thick section little radiation arrives, so only the fast film builds readable density; behind the thin section the fast film overshoots to black while the slow film lands in the readable range.

checkQuestions.question: Under codes that permit composite viewing of a two-film load, the films are interpreted:
options: Separately, and the densities are averaged on paper
Superimposed on the viewer, with each film also required to meet its own minimum density
Superimposed, with no density requirement on the individual films
Only if the films are of different speeds
checkQuestions.explanation: Composite viewing reads the pair superimposed so their densities add, but the code also sets a minimum density for each individual film so that neither sheet is too thin to contribute real information.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiography Technique Course 6.3–6.5
Weld radiography practice per codes such as ASME Section V (panoramic IQI spacing, composite viewing density minimums)
General pipeline radiography practice: internal crawler units with rod-anode panoramic tubes
